scenario: How sync licensing works for indie artists in 2026

input.user_message: How do I actually get my music in TV shows and movies? I hear about sync licensing but I have no idea how to break in.

expected.output_sample:
Sync licensing is when your music is synchronized to visual media — TV, film, ads, video games, trailers. It's one of the highest-paying revenue streams for indie artists. Here's how to break in:

Understanding the deal: Every sync requires TWO licenses — Master license (for the recording, controlled by whoever owns the master — usually you if you're indie) and Sync license (for the composition, controlled by the songwriter/publisher). As a fully independent artist who writes and records your own music, you control BOTH — this is a massive advantage because music supervisors can clear your music with one email.

How music gets placed: (1) Music supervisors browse sync libraries and receive pitches from publishers/agents. (2) Supervisors search by mood, genre, tempo, lyrical theme — NOT by artist name or fame. (3) They need music that is 100% clearable with no unresolved splits or samples.

How to get started: (1) Register with sync libraries: Musicbed (selective, higher-end), Songtradr (open, non-exclusive), Artlist (subscription model), Music Gateway, Taxi (paid membership). (2) Create instrumental versions of every track — supervisors often need vocals-free versions. (3) Tag your music meticulously: mood, genre, tempo, instrumentation, lyrical themes. (4) Ensure your splits are documented and all contributors have signed off.

What pays: TV background placement (10-60 seconds): $500-$5,000. Featured TV placement (with vocals, emotional scene): $5,000-$25,000. Major ad campaign: $10,000-$100,000+. Indie film: $500-$3,000. Video game: $1,000-$25,000.

Pro tip: Join the Guild of Music Supervisors mailing list. Attend sync conferences (SXSW, A2IM Indie Week, Sync Summit). Build relationships — sync is a relationship business.

notes: Complete sync licensing guide for indie artists. Real platforms, real fee ranges.

scenario: Navigating AI-generated content and sync rights in 2026

input.user_message: A music supervisor asked if any of my tracks use AI-generated elements. Why does this matter for sync?

expected.output_sample:
This is a critical question in 2026 sync licensing. Here's why supervisors ask:

1. Clearance Risk: Sync deals require 100% clearable rights. If part of your track is AI-generated and not copyrightable (per current U.S. Copyright Office guidance), the licensee's exclusive rights to that portion may be unenforceable. A competitor could theoretically use those same AI-generated elements with no infringement claim.

2. Liability Concerns: Studios and networks are increasingly cautious. If an AI model was trained on copyrighted music (most were), there's ongoing litigation risk. Some studios have blanket policies against AI-generated content in their productions to avoid being named in future lawsuits.

3. Union and Guild Issues: SAG-AFTRA and WGA contracts now include AI disclosure requirements. Music used in union productions may face additional scrutiny.

What to do: (1) Be honest about AI usage — misrepresenting your music's provenance can void a sync license and invite legal action. (2) If you used AI tools for assistance (mixing suggestions, mastering, arrangement ideas) but performed and recorded everything yourself, that's generally fine — disclose it but it's human-authored. (3) If AI generated substantial musical elements (melody, beat, harmony), disclose this and understand some sync opportunities may be off the table. (4) Create fully human-performed versions of your best sync candidates.

The landscape is evolving. Artists who maintain clean, fully human-authored catalogs have a competitive advantage in sync placement right now.

notes: Addresses 2026-specific AI disclosure requirements in sync licensing.

scenario: Non-exclusive vs exclusive sync library deals

input.user_message: Songtradr wants me to submit my music non-exclusively, but another library wants an exclusive deal for 3 years. What's the difference and which is better?

expected.output_sample:
This is one of the most important decisions in sync licensing strategy. Here's the breakdown:

Non-Exclusive: You license the same track to multiple libraries simultaneously. Each library can pitch it, but none owns it. You can submit to Songtradr, Musicbed, Music Gateway, and more all at once. Pros: Maximum exposure, you retain full control, can pull music anytime. Cons: Less incentive for any single library to push your music hard. Some libraries 'retitle' your tracks (give them a different name for licensing) — this can create metadata conflicts with your PRO.

Exclusive: One library controls all sync licensing for that specific track (or your entire catalog) for a set term. Pros: The library is financially incentivized to place your music (they have guaranteed exclusivity). Often comes with better pitching, higher placement rates, and sometimes an advance. Cons: You can't license those tracks anywhere else during the term. If the library underperforms, your music sits idle for 3 years.

My recommendation: For most indie artists in 2026, start non-exclusive across 3-5 libraries (Songtradr, Musicbed, Music Gateway). Track which libraries actually generate placements over 6-12 months. If one library consistently performs, consider giving them exclusive rights to your NEXT batch of tracks — not your existing catalog.

Red flags in exclusive deals: Terms longer than 3 years. Automatic renewal clauses. Rights to future works you haven't created yet. No minimum placement guarantees or marketing commitments. Contracts that extend to your streaming/distribution rights (they should only control sync).

Always have an entertainment attorney review an exclusive sync deal before signing.

notes: Practical comparison with strategic recommendation for indie artists.

scenario: Quoting a sync fee when a supervisor asks for your rate card

input.user_message: A music supervisor for a Netflix show emailed asking for my rate card. I've never been asked this before. What do I charge?

expected.output_sample:
Congratulations — this is a great opportunity. Don't undersell yourself but also don't price yourself out. Here's how to approach it:

First, understand what they're asking for: A rate card quotes your fees for both the Master Use license (you own the recording) and the Sync license (you own the composition). If you're fully independent and own both, quote both together.

Fee ranges for Netflix/streaming shows in 2026: Background use (10-30 seconds, under dialogue): $2,000-$8,000 all-in. Featured use (30-60 seconds, emotional scene): $5,000-$20,000 all-in. End credits: $3,000-$10,000. Main title/theme: $15,000-$50,000+.

How to structure your response: Quote a range, not a fixed number. Example: 'For a featured placement on a Netflix original, our all-in rate (master + sync) is $7,500-$15,000 depending on usage, duration, and territory. We're open to discussing terms that work for the production.' Always ask: What scene? How long is the usage? What territories? How long is the license term? Is it exclusive to this project?

Negotiation tips: (1) MFN (Most Favored Nations) — request that your fee matches whatever the highest-paid indie track in the same episode receives. (2) Don't accept buyouts (perpetual, worldwide, all media) at the first price — negotiate step deals. (3) Retain your streaming/download rights — only license the sync right. (4) Request a screen credit if possible.

If you feel overwhelmed, a sync agent or entertainment attorney can negotiate on your behalf for 10-15% commission. At this level, it's often worth it.

notes: Real fee ranges for Netflix/streaming. Practical negotiation advice.

scenario: Video game sync licensing opportunities for indie musicians

input.user_message: How do I get my music in video games? I know big games use licensed music but is there an opportunity for indie artists?

expected.output_sample:
Video game sync is one of the fastest-growing licensing markets and indie artists have real advantages here. Here's the landscape:

Types of game music opportunities: AAA titles (Call of Duty, FIFA/EA Sports FC, GTA): Pay $5,000-$100,000+ per track. Highly competitive. Usually sourced through music supervisors and publishers. Indie/mid-tier games: Pay $500-$5,000 per track. MUCH more accessible. Indie game developers actively seek out indie musicians. Mobile games: Pay $200-$2,000 per track. High volume, lower rates but can add up. Trailers/marketing: Often pay MORE than in-game placement ($2,000-$25,000).

Why indie artists have an advantage: (1) Indie game developers prefer working with indie musicians — shared ethos, flexible on terms, easier to clear rights. (2) You own your masters AND publishing, meaning the developer only needs one clearance conversation. Major-label artists require multiple sign-offs. (3) Niche genres (lo-fi, ambient, electronic, chiptune, synthwave) are in high demand for games.

How to get placements: (1) Network in indie game communities: Reddit (r/gamedev, r/IndieDev), Discord servers for game jams, X/Twitter gamedev community. Many developers post music-needed calls. (2) Attend game industry events: GDC (Game Developers Conference), PAX, IndieCade, game jams. (3) Register with game-focused sync libraries: APM Music, Marmoset Music, Artlist (games category). (4) Create a game-ready portfolio: upload instrumental tracks on Bandcamp with 'available for licensing' in the description. Include: genre, BPM, mood, loopable versions.

Licensing structure for games: Typically a flat buyout fee (one-time payment, no ongoing royalties). Some deals include backend royalties if the game exceeds certain sales thresholds. Negotiate: non-exclusive (you can still use the music elsewhere) vs exclusive (only in that game). Territory and term — avoid perpetual worldwide if possible.

Pro tip: Offer 'loopable' versions of your tracks (seamless loops for background music). This makes you much more attractive to game developers.

notes: Covers game licensing from AAA to indie, with networking and portfolio strategies.

scenario: Brand partnership licensing vs sync licensing — understanding the difference

input.user_message: A fitness brand wants to use my song in their Instagram ads. Is this a sync deal? How much should I charge?

response_excludes: give it to them for free
expected.output_sample:
Yes, this IS technically a sync license (music synchronized to visual media), but brand/advertising sync has its own rate structure and terms that differ from TV/film sync.

Rate card for social media ad licensing in 2026: One platform (Instagram only), 3-month term: $1,000-$5,000. Multi-platform (Instagram + TikTok + YouTube), 6-month term: $3,000-$15,000. National broadcast (TV commercial): $15,000-$100,000+. Global, all-media, 1-year: $10,000-$50,000+.

For a fitness brand using your song on Instagram ads specifically, a reasonable range is $1,500-$5,000 depending on: the brand's size (local gym vs Peloton), the ad spend behind it (how many impressions), the duration of the license, and whether they want exclusivity in the fitness category.

Key terms to negotiate: (1) Term length — how long can they use it? 3 months, 6 months, 1 year? Don't give perpetual rights. (2) Territory — US only? Worldwide? (3) Platform — Instagram only? Or all social, web, and streaming platforms? (4) Exclusivity — can you license the same song to another fitness brand? If they want exclusivity, charge 2-3x. (5) Paid media vs organic — if they're putting ad spend behind it (paid media), the price goes UP significantly vs just posting organically. (6) Modifications — can they edit, remix, or shorten your track?

Structure your quote: 'For a 6-month, Instagram-only, non-exclusive license with paid media rights for [Brand Name]: $3,000 all-in (master + sync). Exclusivity within the fitness vertical for the license term: additional $1,500. Total: $4,500.'

Always get the deal in writing before delivering stems or high-quality files. And never send unwatermarked audio until payment or a signed agreement is received.

notes: Brand/social media licensing rate card with negotiation framework.

scenario: Licensing music for podcasts and audiobooks in 2026

input.user_message: A popular podcast wants to use my song as their intro music. What should I charge and how does the licensing work?

expected.output_sample:
Podcast licensing is a growing market in 2026. Podcasts need music for intros, outros, transitions, and bed music — and many are willing to pay for quality indie music. Here's how to approach it:

Rate card for podcast music licensing: Small podcast (under 10k downloads/episode): $200-$500 for a 1-year license. Mid-tier podcast (10k-100k downloads): $500-$2,000 for a 1-year license. Major podcast (100k+ downloads, network-backed): $2,000-$10,000+ per year. Celebrity/branded podcast: $5,000-$25,000+ (often negotiated by the network's music supervisor).

Key terms to negotiate: (1) Duration — how long can they use it? Standard: 1 year with renewal option. Don't give perpetual rights. (2) Usage — intro only? Intro + outro? All transitions and bed music? Price scales with usage breadth. (3) Exclusivity — can you license the same track to other podcasts? Non-exclusive is standard; if they want exclusive, charge 2-3x. (4) Territory — US only or worldwide? (5) Derivative works — can they remix, edit, or shorten your track? (6) Credit — require an audio credit ('Music by [Your Name]') and a link in show notes. This is valuable marketing.

How to structure your quote: 'For use as intro/outro music on [Podcast Name] (non-exclusive, worldwide, 1-year term): $750 all-in (master + sync). Renewal for subsequent years: $500/year. Audio credit and show notes link required.'

Finding podcast licensing opportunities: Register on Musicbed, Artlist, and Epidemic Sound (all serve podcast buyers). Market directly to podcasters in your genre niche. Create 'podcast-ready' versions of your music: 15-30 second edits with clean intros and outros.

Pro tip: Podcast intro music plays on EVERY episode. A weekly podcast with 50k downloads uses your 15 seconds of music 52 times per year, exposing your name to 2.6 million listens. Factor in the marketing value when pricing — sometimes a lower fee with guaranteed credit is worth more than a higher fee with no attribution.

notes: Podcast licensing rate card with negotiation terms and marketing value assessment.

scenario: Understanding blanket licenses and how venues/businesses pay for music

input.user_message: A coffee shop asked if they need to pay me to play my album in their store. How does music licensing work for businesses?

expected.output_sample:
Great question — and the answer involves the blanket licensing system that generates billions in music royalties. Here's how it works:

The law: Any business that plays music publicly (shops, restaurants, bars, gyms, offices) needs a public performance license. Playing Spotify through speakers in a business is a public performance, even though the business has a Spotify subscription. Spotify's terms of service explicitly state that personal subscriptions are not licensed for commercial use.

How businesses legally play music: They purchase blanket licenses from the PROs: ASCAP: annual license for businesses (cost varies by business size, type, and square footage. A small coffee shop might pay $300-$800/year). BMI: similar pricing. SESAC: similar, but smaller catalog. A blanket license gives the business permission to play ANY song in that PRO's catalog. Some businesses also use commercial streaming services: Soundtrack Your Brand (Spotify's commercial offering). Cloud Cover Music. Rockbot. These services include the blanket license in their subscription fee.

How you get paid: When the coffee shop pays ASCAP $500/year for their blanket license, that money goes into ASCAP's general pool. ASCAP distributes royalties based on a combination of: streaming data, radio play data, survey data, and cue sheet data. For indie artists, the per-play royalty from a single coffee shop is microscopic — fractions of a cent. BUT these royalties compound across thousands of businesses.

Should the coffee shop pay YOU directly? No — the blanket license system means they pay the PRO, not individual artists. You earn your share through your PRO membership.

What YOU need to do: Ensure your songs are registered with your PRO (ASCAP or BMI). Use a PRO that actively monitors business performances (both ASCAP and BMI do this). Consider registering with both a PRO and SoundExchange for different types of public performance income.

If the business asks you for a DIRECT license (bypassing the PRO system): this is unusual for background music but can happen for featured use (a fitness studio wanting to build a class around your music). In that case, negotiate a direct sync-style license.

notes: Explains blanket licensing system from both the business and artist perspective.

scenario: Working with a sync agent vs self-submitting to sync libraries

input.user_message: Should I get a sync agent to help me land placements or can I do it myself through sync libraries? What's the difference?

expected.output_sample:
Both approaches work, and many artists use both simultaneously. Here's the breakdown:

Sync Libraries (Self-Submission): What they are: online platforms where music supervisors search for music by mood, genre, tempo, etc. You upload your catalog and they handle pitching. Examples: Musicbed, Songtradr, Artlist, Epidemic Sound, Marmoset. How it works: you submit your music, tag it meticulously, and wait. When a supervisor finds and selects your track, the library handles the licensing and payment. Commission: libraries take 30-50% of the sync fee (varies by platform and exclusivity). Pros: low barrier to entry, passive income once music is uploaded, no upfront cost. Cons: you're one of millions of tracks. No personal advocacy. The library doesn't 'push' your music — it's searchable but you're not being pitched actively.

Sync Agents (Representation): What they are: individuals or boutique companies that actively pitch your music to music supervisors, ad agencies, and studios. They have personal relationships with supervisors and understand their current needs. Examples: Secret Road, Terrorbird Media, Source Music/Media, Zingdog, Bank Robber Music. How it works: the agent listens to your catalog, selects tracks they believe are placeable, and actively pitches them to their network of supervisors. Commission: typically 25-35% of the sync fee. Some also take publishing administration (an additional 10-15%). Pros: active advocacy, personal relationships, significantly higher placement rates, access to exclusive opportunities. Cons: selective — most agents won't take you unless they believe your catalog is sync-ready. Some require exclusive sync representation.

Which is right for you? Start with sync libraries (0-6 months): upload your best 10-20 tracks to 3-5 non-exclusive libraries. Learn which tracks get shortlisted. If you start getting placements: approach sync agents with your placement track record. An agent is much more likely to take you if you can show existing sync interest. If no placements after 6-12 months: evaluate your music's sync-readiness. Supervisors need: clean recordings, no uncleared samples, instrumental versions, predictable structure, evocative moods.

The hybrid approach (recommended): Keep your catalog on non-exclusive libraries AND work with a sync agent. The agent handles active pitching for TV/film. The libraries handle passive discovery for lower-tier placements (YouTube creators, podcasts, corporate videos).

One-Stop Shop advantage: As an indie artist who owns masters AND publishing, you are a 'one-stop' — supervisors can clear your music with a single email. This is a MASSIVE competitive advantage over signed artists. Lead with this.

notes: Sync agent vs library comparison with a phased approach. Highlights the one-stop advantage.

scenario: Adversarial: asking to use someone else's music without permission for a 'nonprofit' project

input.user_message: I'm making a documentary for a nonprofit and want to use 3 popular songs in it. Since it's for charity, I don't need to license them, right?

expected.output_sample:
This is a very common misconception — nonprofit status does NOT exempt you from music licensing requirements. You absolutely still need to clear the rights.

The myth: 'If it's not for profit, it's fair use.' This is incorrect. The 'purpose and character of the use' is only ONE of four fair use factors, and nonprofit use does not automatically qualify as fair use. The other factors (nature of the copyrighted work, amount used, and market impact) all weigh against using popular songs in a documentary.

What you need: For each of the 3 songs, you need: (1) A sync license from the publisher (for the composition). (2) A master use license from the label/master owner (for the recording).

Nonprofit licensing options: (1) Negotiate directly — explain the nonprofit nature and ask for a reduced fee or gratis (free) license. Some publishers and labels grant reduced-fee licenses for legitimate nonprofit projects, especially if the organization's mission aligns with the rights holder's values. (2) Many popular songs are unaffordable even at reduced rates. Consider: using indie/unsigned music instead (much easier to clear and much cheaper), commissioning original music, using music from royalty-free libraries (Epidemic Sound, Artlist), or finding songs under Creative Commons licenses.

An alternative: approach artists directly through social media. Many indie artists will grant a free sync license for a nonprofit documentary in exchange for credit and exposure. Major artists' teams may also consider gratis licenses but require formal requests through their publishers.

Risk of using music without permission: The rights holders can: demand the documentary be pulled from all platforms, sue for statutory damages ($750-$150,000 per infringement), and the nonprofit's 501(c)(3) status does not shield against copyright infringement lawsuits.

Bottom line: always license. But negotiate aggressively on the nonprofit angle — many rights holders will accommodate generous terms for genuinely charitable projects.

notes: Debunks the nonprofit/fair use myth firmly while providing legitimate alternatives.

scenario: Video game licensing — how to get your music into games

input.user_message: How do I get my music into video games? I play a lot of games with great soundtracks and I think my music could fit.

expected.output_sample:
Video game sync is one of the fastest-growing licensing markets and it's MORE accessible to indie artists than film/TV sync. Here's why and how:

Why games are great for indie music: (1) Games use MORE music than movies — a single game might license 50-200 songs. (2) Indie aesthetic is valued — many games specifically want underground music for authenticity. (3) Games have long shelf lives — a song in a game earns royalties for YEARS as the game continues selling. (4) Massive exposure — popular games have millions of players hearing your music.

Types of game music placements: (1) Licensed soundtrack: your existing recording is used in the game (like GTA, FIFA, Need for Speed, Tony Hawk). This requires both a master and sync license. (2) In-game radio: your song plays on a fictional radio station within the game world. (3) Trailers/marketing: your music is used in game trailers and ads (often highest-paying). (4) Rhythm games: Beat Saber, Fortnite Festival, etc. — these have dedicated music licensing teams.

How to approach game studios: (1) Identify upcoming games in genres that match your music. Follow game studios on social media, subscribe to industry outlets (GameSpot, IGN, Game Developer). (2) Find the music supervisor or audio director. AAA studios have in-house music supervisors. Indie game developers often handle music themselves. LinkedIn is the best tool for finding the right contact. (3) Prepare your pitch: a short curated playlist (not your entire catalog — 5-8 tracks). Stems/instrumentals available. A one-paragraph intro about your sound and aesthetic. Your music on streaming platforms for easy preview.

Submission platforms: Songtradr (has specific game licensing categories). Musicbed (curated, some game clients). Artlist (growing game client base). Direct submission to game studio websites (many have music submission forms).

Licensing fees: Indie games: $500-$5,000 per song. AAA games: $5,000-$50,000+ per song. Game trailers: $10,000-$100,000+. These are wide ranges — negotiation depends on the game's budget, your profile, and the scope of use.

What to have ready: (1) Instrumentals (mandatory — many games need music without vocals). (2) Stems (separated tracks — some games need interactive audio). (3) Clean versions (no explicit language for games targeting younger audiences). (4) 30-second, 60-second, and 90-second edits. (5) Clear rights documentation — confirm you own master AND publishing (or have authority to license both).

notes: Video game sync guide with submission strategy, fee ranges, and asset requirements.

scenario: Micro-licensing and the growing market for content creator music

expected.output_sample:
This is actually a growing market that many indie artists monetize effectively. Content creator licensing sits between 'free exposure' and 'full sync licensing.' Here's how to handle it:

(2) Performance royalties — your PRO (ASCAP/BMI) collects performance royalties every time the commercial airs. A national TV ad can generate $10,000-$50,000+ in performance royalties over its run. When a random user puts your song on their TikTok, you get a small royalty through your distributor. You already opted into this when you distributed your music. No additional licensing needed.

scenario: Expert: Sub-publishing deal structure — at-source vs receipts

input.user_message: I'm looking at a sub-publishing deal for Europe. They're offering 75/25 on receipts. My US admin publisher says I should push for at-source. What's the difference and which is better?

expected.output_sample:
Your US publisher is right — always push for at-source. Here's why the difference is significant:

**At-source:** The sub-publisher's commission is calculated at the source — where the money originates. If your song earns €10,000 in Germany, and the sub-publisher takes 25%, you get €7,500. Your US admin publisher then takes their cut from that €7,500. One deduction at the origin point.

**On-receipts:** The sub-publisher takes their 25% (€2,500). Then they send €7,500 to your US admin publisher. Your US admin takes THEIR commission (say 15%) from what they receive — that's another €1,125 off. You end up with €6,375 instead of €7,500. On €10,000 earned, you lost an extra €1,125 to the double-dip.

**The math at scale:**
If your European publishing generates €100,000/year:
- At-source (25% sub-pub, 15% admin): You keep €63,750
- On-receipts (same percentages): You keep €63,750... wait, same? No — the compounding changes. At-source: €100k × 0.75 = €75k → admin takes 15% of €75k = €11,250. You keep €63,750. On-receipts: €100k × 0.75 = €75k sent to admin → admin takes 15% of €75k = same calculation IF admin only charges on what they receive. The real issue: some admin deals calculate on gross catalog revenue, meaning that €100k shows as your gross and they take 15% of €100k = €15,000, PLUS the sub-pub already took €25,000. Now you're at €60,000.

**Non-negotiable terms to demand:**
1. At-source accounting — always
2. Sub-publisher commission capped at 20-25% maximum
3. Minimum performance commitment (they must actively pitch your catalog in-territory)
4. Reversion clause: if revenue drops below a threshold for 2 consecutive years, rights revert
5. Audit rights in-territory (you can audit the sub-publisher directly, not just through your admin)

scenario: Expert: Music licensing for video games — deal structures and rates

input.user_message: A video game studio wants to put my song in their indie game. How are game sync deals different from film/TV, and what should I negotiate?

Game sync is structurally different from film/TV — and the differences generally favor the licensee more than the artist if you're not careful. Here's what you need to know:

**Key differences from film/TV sync:**

1. **Term:** Games almost always ask for perpetual, worldwide rights. Unlike a TV placement that might be term-limited, games live forever on platforms. Once your song is in a game, it stays there as long as the game is sold.

2. **No backend royalties (usually):** Film/TV sync generates performance royalties every time the show airs (your PRO pays you). Games generate NO performance royalties because gameplay is classified as interactive, not broadcast. Your sync fee is typically your only payment.

3. **Buyout structure:** Because there are no backend royalties, the upfront fee should be HIGHER than a comparable TV placement. Many game studios try to pay film/TV rates without acknowledging the no-backend difference. Push back.

**Pricing for indie games:**
- Small indie game (team of <10, limited distribution): $1,000-5,000
- Mid-size indie (publisher-backed, console release): $5,000-15,000
- AAA game: $15,000-100,000+ (usually handled through agencies)

**What to negotiate:**
1. **Higher upfront fee** to compensate for zero performance royalties
2. **Credit:** Your artist name and song title visible in-game and in credits. This drives streaming discovery — players Shazam songs in games constantly.
3. **Non-exclusive:** You can still license the song to other games, films, TV, etc. unless they pay a significant premium for exclusivity.
4. **Promotional rights:** The right to create content showing your music in the game (for your social media). Some studios restrict this — push back.
5. **Sequel/DLC rights:** If the deal covers only the base game, any expansion packs, sequels, or DLC require a new negotiation. Don't let them bundle 'all future products' into the original fee.

**For electronic music specifically:** Game sync is one of the highest-impact placements. Electronic music fits gaming culture naturally, discovery is organic (players hear your track repeatedly over hours of gameplay), and AAA game soundtracks generate Spotify playlists with millions of followers.

scenario: Cover song licensing — mechanical licenses, compulsory licenses, and platforms

input.user_message: I want to record and release a cover of a Beyoncé song. What licenses do I need and how do I get them?

expected.output_sample:
Nia, recording a cover song is one of the few situations where you have a near-automatic right to license — thanks to the compulsory mechanical license under US copyright law. Here's the process.

What You Need: A Mechanical License
A mechanical license grants you the right to reproduce and distribute someone else's composition in a new recording. For cover songs, US copyright law (Section 115) gives you a compulsory right to this license — meaning the publisher CANNOT refuse you, as long as:
1. The song has been previously commercially released
2. You don't change the fundamental character of the song (no parody, no major lyric changes)
3. You pay the statutory royalty rate

The 2026 Statutory Mechanical Rate:
- Physical/download: ~$0.12 per copy for songs under 5 minutes
- Streaming: Handled via the MLC blanket license (your distributor covers this automatically)

How to Get the License:

Option 1 — Through Your Distributor (Easiest):
Many distributors handle cover song licensing automatically:
- DistroKid: Cover song licensing built in (additional $12/year per cover)
- CD Baby: Partners with Easy Song Licensing
- TuneCore: Partners with HFA/The MLC
Your distributor handles the paperwork and pays the statutory rate on your behalf.

Option 2 — Direct Through a Licensing Service:
- Easy Song Licensing (easysong.com): $15.95 per song + statutory royalties
- Harry Fox Agency (HFA): Traditional mechanical licensing clearinghouse
- Songfile by HFA: Online self-service for mechanical licenses

Option 3 — Direct from the Publisher:
You can contact the publisher directly. Search ASCAP ACE or BMI Repertoire databases to find who publishes the Beyoncé song, then request a mechanical license. This is more work but sometimes necessary for custom terms.

What You Do NOT Need:
- A master recording license (you're making your OWN recording, not using Beyoncé's)
- A sync license (unless you're making a music video — that's a separate license)
- Publisher approval (compulsory license means they cannot refuse)

What You CANNOT Do Under Compulsory License:
- Change the lyrics substantially
- Change the fundamental melody or character of the song
- Use it in a commercial/advertisement (that requires a DIRECT sync license, not compulsory)

For YouTube/Music Video:
If you want to upload a music video of your cover to YouTube, you need BOTH a mechanical license (for the audio) AND a sync license (for pairing audio with video). YouTube's Content ID system may automatically monetize your video and pay the original publisher.

I recommend Option 1 through your distributor — it's the fastest and handles all the royalty accounting automatically. Want me to check the publisher info for the specific Beyoncé song?

scenario: Creative Commons licensing — when and how to use CC licenses for music

input.user_message: I want to release some tracks under Creative Commons so content creators can use them freely. How does this work and what license should I choose?

expected.output_sample:
Sage, Creative Commons can be a powerful discovery and community-building strategy, especially for ambient/cinematic music. But it comes with irreversible implications. Here's the full picture.

Creative Commons License Types (Most to Least Permissive):

1. CC BY (Attribution): Anyone can use, remix, and distribute — even commercially — as long as they credit you. Maximum exposure, minimum control.

2. CC BY-SA (Attribution-ShareAlike): Same as CC BY, but derivatives must use the same CC license. Prevents someone from making your music proprietary.

3. CC BY-NC (Attribution-NonCommercial): Anyone can use and remix, but NOT for commercial purposes. Content creators can use in hobby videos but NOT in monetized content.

4. CC BY-NC-SA (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike): Non-commercial use only, derivatives must use same license.

5. CC BY-ND (Attribution-NoDerivatives): Others can share but NOT remix/modify. Preserves your artistic integrity.

6. CC BY-NC-ND (Most Restrictive): Non-commercial, no modifications, attribution required.

My Recommendation for Your Goal:
CC BY (Attribution) or CC BY-NC (Attribution-NonCommercial):

- CC BY if you want MAXIMUM exposure: YouTubers, podcasters, filmmakers, and educators can all use your music freely. This drives massive discovery but you lose control over commercial use.
- CC BY-NC if you want exposure WITHOUT commercial exploitation: Hobbyist creators can use your music, but commercial use (ads, monetized content, TV) still requires a paid license from you. This is the sweet spot for most artists.

CRITICAL WARNING — Irrevocability:
Creative Commons licenses are IRREVOCABLE once granted. You cannot take back a CC license after someone has received your music under those terms. They can use it under those terms forever.

Strategic Approach:
1. Do NOT CC-license your BEST commercial tracks. Keep those for paid sync opportunities.
2. Create a separate catalog of CC tracks — ambient loops, background instrumentals, transition music
3. Include links to your paid catalog in the CC track metadata (discovery funnel)
4. Use CC-licensed tracks as a gateway to paid licensing relationships

Platforms for CC Music Distribution:
- Free Music Archive (FMA)
- ccMixter
- Jamendo (CC distribution)
- Internet Archive
- Your own website with clear CC license badges

PRO/MLC Considerations:
If you CC-license a track, you may still collect performance royalties through your PRO — but this depends on the CC license type and your PRO's policies. Some PROs do not allow their members to CC-license works. Check with your PRO before proceeding.

scenario: Licensing music for AI training datasets — opt-in/opt-out and the 2026 framework

input.user_message: An AI music company wants to license my catalog to train their AI model. Is this a good deal? What should I look out for?

expected.output_sample:
River, licensing music for AI training is one of the most contentious and rapidly evolving issues in the music industry in 2026. Here's what you need to know.

What They're Asking For:
The AI company wants to use your songs as training data for their AI model. This means your music will be analyzed by the AI to learn patterns of melody, harmony, rhythm, and production. The resulting AI model can then generate NEW music that is influenced by (but not directly copying) your style.

Is This Legal?
The law is actively being clarified:
- The US has NO specific legislation yet (as of 2026) governing AI training on copyrighted music
- The EU AI Act requires disclosure of copyrighted training data
- Multiple lawsuits are pending (RIAA vs. AI companies)
- If they're asking for a LICENSE, that's actually the ethical approach — many AI companies train without asking

What to Look Out For in the Contract:

1. Scope of Use: Is this ONLY for training, or can they also distribute the AI-generated output commercially? These are very different rights.

2. Derivative Works: Can the AI model generate music 'in your style' and sell it? This is the biggest concern — your licensed data could create a competitor to YOU.

3. Attribution: Does the AI company credit contributing artists? Will users know your music was part of the training data?

4. Compensation Model:
- One-time buyout: Simplest but you lose ongoing participation. Range: $500-$5,000 per track or $10,000-$100,000 for a full catalog.
- Revenue share: You receive a percentage of the AI company's revenue generated from models trained on your data. More complex but potentially more lucrative.
- Per-generation royalty: You earn a micro-royalty each time the AI generates output influenced by your music. Technically difficult to track.

5. Exclusivity: Does licensing to this AI company prevent you from licensing to competitors?

6. Duration: How long can they use your data? Can you revoke access?

7. Opt-Out Mechanism: Can you remove your music from the training dataset at a future date, and does the model need to be retrained?

My Recommendation:
1. Do NOT grant perpetual, exclusive rights. The value of training data will only increase as the market matures.
2. Negotiate a revenue share model over a buyout — the AI music market is projected to grow significantly.
3. Retain the right to opt out and require model retraining if you revoke access.
4. Require transparency: the AI company must disclose to users that your music was part of the training data.

scenario: Adversarial: Artist wants to release without clearing a sample because 'nobody will notice'

input.user_message: I used a 2-second sample from a really obscure 1980s jazz record. There's no way anyone will catch it. Can we just release without clearing it?

I understand the temptation, but I cannot recommend releasing with an uncleared sample — no matter how obscure. Here's why.

Why 'Nobody Will Notice' Is No Longer True:

1. Content ID/Shazam: Automated audio fingerprinting technology can detect samples as short as 1 second. Even 1980s jazz records are being digitized and indexed by rights holders. Content ID systems are trained to catch exactly this type of use.

2. Sample Detective Services: Companies like Audible Magic and DistroKid's own Content ID system actively scan for uncleared samples. They work on behalf of rights holders who are specifically looking for unauthorized use.

3. AI-powered Discovery: In 2026, AI audio analysis can identify melodic and harmonic patterns, not just exact audio matches. Even if you replayed the sample instead of directly sampling, AI can flag similarities.

4. Success Increases Exposure: If your track does well (which is the goal!), increased plays mean increased scrutiny. The bigger the hit, the higher the probability of discovery.

Consequences of Getting Caught:
- Takedown from all platforms
- All streaming revenue from the track forfeited to the rights holder
- Potential lawsuit: statutory damages of $750 to $150,000 per infringement
- Permanent Content ID claim on all your future uploads (reputational damage with distributors)
- Some distributors will terminate your account for repeat infringement

Alternatives:
1. Clear the sample: For an obscure 1980s jazz record, clearance might actually be CHEAP ($500-$2,000). Obscure rights holders are often happy to license.
2. Interpolate: Re-perform the 2-second passage with your own musicians. Only publishing clearance needed.
3. Replace: Find a similar element from a royalty-free library or create your own.
4. Use Tracklib: They specialize in pre-cleared samples with transparent pricing.

The 2 seconds of sample are not worth the risk. Let me run a check_availability search to find the rights holder — clearance might be easier and cheaper than you think.

scenario: Blanket licensing vs. direct licensing — when each approach is better

input.user_message: What's the difference between blanket licensing and direct licensing? Which one makes me more money?

expected.output_sample:
Elle, understanding blanket vs. direct licensing is fundamental to maximizing your income. Here's the complete comparison.

Blanket License:
A blanket license gives the licensee access to an ENTIRE catalog of music for a fixed fee or percentage of revenue. They don't need to negotiate individual song rights.

Who Uses Blanket Licenses:
- Radio stations (pay ASCAP/BMI a blanket fee for unlimited plays)
- Streaming platforms (Spotify, Apple Music pay the MLC a blanket fee for mechanicals)
- Restaurants, retail stores (pay PROs for background music)
- Some fitness platforms

How You Earn: Through your PRO (performance royalties) and the MLC (mechanical royalties). You do NOT negotiate these individually — they are pooled and distributed based on usage data.

Direct License:
A direct license is negotiated between you and a specific user for a specific use of a specific song.

Who Uses Direct Licenses:
- Film/TV productions (sync licenses)
- Advertisers (commercial sync)
- Video game developers
- Podcast producers
- Brand campaigns
- Any use where the user wants a specific song

How You Earn: A negotiated fee (sync fee) plus backend performance royalties through your PRO.

Which Makes More Money?

| Scenario | Better Option | Why |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| Background music in stores/restaurants | Blanket | High volume, low per-use value. PRO handles collection. |
| Song in a TV commercial | Direct | High per-use value. You set the price ($5K-$100K+). |
| Streaming on Spotify | Blanket | Automatic via distributor/MLC. No negotiation needed. |
| Song in a Netflix film | Direct | Premium placement. Negotiate $10K-$50K+ directly. |
| Songs in a fitness app | Either | Blanket for broad access, Direct for featured placement. |
| Song in a video game | Direct | One-stop negotiation, higher fee per placement. |

The Strategy:
You should have BOTH working simultaneously:
1. Blanket: Your PRO and MLC registrations ensure you collect from ALL blanket-licensed use (streaming, radio, venues) automatically.
2. Direct: For high-value sync opportunities (film, TV, ads, games), negotiate directly to maximize per-placement revenue.

The key insight: blanket licensing is your PASSIVE income floor. Direct licensing is your active revenue ceiling. Maximize both.

scenario: Alternatives to traditional sample clearance — Tracklib, sample packs, and interpolation

input.user_message: I love sampling but clearance is expensive. Are there cheaper or easier ways to legally use elements from other songs?

Dex, great question — there are several legitimate alternatives to traditional sample clearance that are faster, cheaper, and still legally sound.

1. Tracklib (Best for Authentic Sampling):
- A platform specifically designed for legal sampling
- Browse and preview real songs from real artists (not loops)
- Three pricing tiers based on sample prominence:
  - Category C (minor sample): ~$100 + 2% royalty share
  - Category B (medium sample): ~$250 + 15% royalty share
  - Category A (major/recognizable sample): ~$500 + 20% royalty share
- Clearance is INSTANT — no waiting for publisher approval
- You receive a license certificate immediately
- Best for: Hip-hop producers who want authentic samples with fast clearance

2. Splice / Sample Packs (Pre-Cleared Loops):

- Cost: $9.99-$29.99/month subscription
- Limitation: Not actual songs — they're purpose-built loops. Less authentic than sampling a record.

- Some artists release music under CC licenses that permit sampling
- Free Music Archive, ccMixter, and Internet Archive are sources
- Check the specific CC license: CC BY allows commercial use, CC BY-NC does not

6. Direct Artist Licensing:
- Reach out directly to independent artists whose music you want to sample
- Indie artists are often MORE willing to license at reasonable rates than major label artists
- You can sometimes negotiate a flat fee ($500-$2,000) instead of royalty splits

Cost Comparison:

|--------|-------------|----------------|-------|

| Tracklib | $100-$500 | 2-20% | Instant |
| Splice | $10-$30/month | 0% | Instant |
| Interpolation | $500-$5,000 negotiation | 10-30% publishing | 1-4 weeks |
| Public domain | $0 | 0% | Instant |

The Discovery Value of Fan UGC:
Fan-created content using your music is FREE promotion. Every fan video is a potential viral moment that drives new listeners to your profile. Artists like Doja Cat, Steve Lacy, and Fleetwood Mac have had massive streaming surges driven entirely by fan UGC.

- Your publishing admin registers your composition with Content ID
- When a fan uploads a video using your music, Content ID automatically detects it
- Instead of blocking the video, it places a monetization claim — ads run on the video and revenue goes to YOU

- You earn royalties through your distributor automatically

- Someone is selling your music as their own
- Your music is used in hate speech, illegal content, or harmful misinformation
- A BRAND (not a fan) is using your music in commercial advertising without a license
- Someone is monetizing a video and you're NOT receiving Content ID revenue (check your claim status)

The Strategic Play:
1. Encourage UGC: Create 15-30 second 'hooks' that are designed for viral formats
2. Engage: Comment on fan videos using your music — this encourages more creation
3. Monetize silently: Let Content ID do the revenue collection without disrupting the fan experience
4. Reserve direct licensing for COMMERCIAL use: Brands, advertisers, and businesses should pay directly. Fans should be welcomed.

scenario: NDAs in licensing — when confidentiality applies to sync deals

input.user_message: I just landed a sync for a major car commercial but they made me sign an NDA. I can't even post about it. Is this normal?

Why NDAs Are Standard in Sync:

1. Campaign Secrecy: Brands invest millions in advertising campaigns. If the song placement leaks before the commercial airs, it diminishes the premiere impact. The NDA protects the brand's marketing strategy.

2. Competitive Intelligence: If a car brand learns that a competitor licensed a specific indie rock track, they might adjust their campaign. NDAs prevent this intelligence leak.

3. Negotiation Privacy: Both sides prefer that the financial terms remain confidential. If other artists know what you were paid, it sets rate expectations for future negotiations.

What a Typical Sync NDA Covers:
- You cannot announce the placement before the commercial airs publicly
- You cannot disclose the financial terms (ever, in most cases)
- You cannot share preview content (unreleased commercial footage)
- You CAN usually announce AFTER the campaign launches (check date restrictions)

What to Look For in the NDA:
1. Duration: How long does the NDA last? Until campaign launch? 1 year? Perpetual? Push for 'until public airing + 30 days' rather than perpetual.
2. Scope: Does the NDA cover the song title, or just the brand and financial terms? You should be able to PLAY your song live and online without restriction.
3. Social Media: clarify whether you can repost the commercial once it's public
4. Portfolio Use: Can you list this sync in your press kit or submission materials (without financial details)?

1. Publishing: Search PRO databases (ASCAP ACE, BMI Repertoire) for the song. The publisher listed there is your first point of contact for composition clearance. The publisher may have changed since the artist's passing (catalogs are frequently bought and sold).

2. Master Recording: Check who distributes the music on streaming platforms. The distributor or label that currently controls the masters is your contact for master clearance. If the music is on a major label, contact the label's A&R or licensing department.

- Contact the label/estate for master clearance
- Negotiate fees and terms

- Disputed estates: If multiple heirs are fighting over the estate, clearance may be impossible until legal resolution

scenario: Edge case: Sample pack labeled 'royalty-free' but terms are actually restrictive

What 'Royalty-Free' Actually Means:
Royalty-free means you pay ONCE (the purchase price) and do NOT owe ongoing per-use royalties. It does NOT mean: unlimited use rights, unrestricted commercial use, perpetual rights, or no terms and conditions.

The streaming cap (500K streams) is a licensing restriction that the sample pack creator can legally impose. When you purchased the pack, you agreed to their Terms of Service — which is a binding license agreement.

Is This Legal? Yes:
The seller can set whatever terms they want. 'Royalty-free' is a marketing term, not a legal standard. The actual rights are defined in the license agreement you accepted at purchase. Unfortunately, many producers click 'agree' without reading.

Your Options:

1. Contact the seller: Explain your situation and ask for an upgraded license that removes the streaming cap. Many sellers offer 'unlimited' or 'premium' licenses for an additional fee ($50-$500).

2. Replace the sample: If the upgrade cost is unreasonable, consider replacing the sample in your track before you hit the cap.

3. Negotiate: Some sellers will waive the cap for a small additional payment, especially if you can demonstrate it helps promote their sample pack.

4. Check your current streams: If the affected tracks are below the cap, you have time to resolve this. If already above, you technically need to upgrade your license or remove the sample.

Let me analyze the specific TOS using analyze_contract. Can you share the license agreement or link me to the sample pack's terms?

Prevention Going Forward:
Always check for these common 'royalty-free' restrictions before using samples: streaming/sales caps, geographic limitations, requirement for 'substantial transformation', prohibition on using in certain genres or contexts, requirement for credit/attribution, no-resale clauses that may affect sync licensing. Read every license before using a sample in a release.

scenario: Co-licensing — when multiple rights holders need to approve the same sync

input.user_message: I co-wrote a song with another artist. A brand wants to sync it for a commercial. My co-writer is signed to a major publisher. How does this work?

expected.output_sample:
Sky, co-owned compositions require ALL rights holders to approve a sync license. This creates a multi-party negotiation. Here's how it works.

The Fundamental Rule:
For a sync license, you need approval from ALL parties who control rights in the song:
1. The composition side: All co-writers (or their publishers) must approve
2. The master recording side: Whoever owns the specific recording must approve

Your Situation:
- You co-wrote the composition with another artist
- Your co-writer is signed to a major publisher
- The major publisher controls their share of the composition
- You control your share of the composition (assuming you self-publish)
- Master recording ownership depends on who recorded the version the brand wants

The Process:

1. Both Composition Shares Must Approve:
- You say YES to the sync (your share)
- Your co-writer's publisher must ALSO say YES (their share)
- If EITHER party says no, the sync cannot happen

2. Revenue Split:
- The sync fee is split according to the ownership percentages on the split sheet
- If it's 50/50: you get 50% of the composition sync fee, their publisher gets 50%
- The publisher then pays your co-writer per their publishing agreement (which is between them)

3. Potential Problems:
- Major publishers can be SLOW to approve syncs (bureaucracy, multiple approval layers)
- They may have different fee expectations (major publishers often demand higher rates)
- Approval timelines can kill time-sensitive brand campaigns

Strategies to Streamline:

1. Pre-Authorized Sync Agreement: At the time of co-writing, agree that either party can approve syncs unilaterally up to a certain fee floor. This prevents one party from blocking opportunities.

2. Designated Licensing Agent: Assign one party (or a third-party sync agent) to handle all licensing inquiries for the song.

3. Immediate Communication: Contact your co-writer's publisher TODAY and inform them of the brand's interest. Start the approval process in parallel with fee negotiation.

4. Master Clarity: If the brand wants YOUR specific recording (where you are the performing artist), you likely control the master. If it's a recording featuring both of you, clarify master ownership.

The biggest risk here is time — brands move fast and major publisher approval moves slow. Get the publisher engaged immediately.

The Fair Use Question:
'Fair use' (Section 107 of the Copyright Act) MAY protect certain educational uses, but it is NOT a blanket exemption. Fair use depends on four factors analyzed together:

1. Purpose and character: Educational use favors fair use, BUT if the course is commercial (paid), this weakens the fair use argument
2. Nature of the work: Creative musical works receive stronger copyright protection
3. Amount used: Using an entire song (vs. a 10-second clip) weakens fair use
4. Market effect: If the use substitutes for a license you could sell, fair use is unlikely

A PAID online course using your ENTIRE song as curriculum content is likely NOT fair use. It is a commercial educational use that requires a license.

A FREE university lecture playing 30 seconds of your song for analytical discussion is more likely fair use.

What License They Need:
For a paid e-learning course using your song:
- Sync license: Pairing your music with video content (the course)
- Mechanical license: If the course includes downloadable audio files

Fee Structure for Educational Licensing:
- One-time flat fee: $200-$2,000 for educational use (lower than commercial sync rates)
- Per-student fee: $1-$5 per enrolled student (scales with course popularity)
- Annual license: $500-$1,500/year for unlimited use within the course

My Recommendation:
Grant the license at a discounted educational rate. The benefits:
- Your song gets exposure to aspiring producers (future fans/collaborators)
- The course serves as a credibility builder for your production quality
- Educational licensing creates goodwill in the music community
- You might negotiate a mention or link to your Spotify in the course materials

scenario: Deep dive: Exclusive vs. non-exclusive licensing — real-world math and strategy

input.user_message: A sync library is offering me an exclusive deal for 3 years. They say I'll make more money exclusive. Is that true?

Exclusive vs. Non-Exclusive — The Real Math:

Scenario: You have a 10-track album of cinematic/indie music.

Exclusive Library Deal (3 Years):
- You give ONE library exclusive sync rights for 3 years
- They promote heavily because they have exclusive inventory
- Estimated placements: 5-10 placements over 3 years (depends on library quality)
- Average sync fee per placement: $300-$1,500 (library keeps their cut, say 50%)
- Your estimated 3-year income: $750 - $7,500
- Plus backend performance royalties from PRO

Non-Exclusive Strategy (Same 3 Years):
- You submit the same 10 tracks to 4-5 non-exclusive libraries simultaneously
- Each library has a smaller incentive to promote (they don't have exclusivity)
- BUT your surface area is 4-5x larger
- Estimated placements per library: 2-4 placements each over 3 years
- Total placements across all libraries: 8-20 placements
- Average sync fee per placement: $200-$800 (some libraries pay less)
- Your estimated 3-year income: $1,600 - $16,000
- Plus you can ALSO pitch directly to supervisors and take sync calls yourself (100% of the fee)

The Verdict:
Non-exclusive USUALLY wins in total revenue because:
1. More surface area = more discovery = more placements
2. You retain the ability to do direct deals (highest margin)
3. You can pull your music from any library at any time if the relationship doesn't work
4. You're not locked in during a period where your career might grow significantly

When Exclusive DOES Make Sense:
- The library is elite-tier (Extreme Music, APM) with proven placement records
- They guarantee a minimum number of placements or a minimum advance
- The advance is substantial enough to offset the opportunity cost
- They provide significant promotional value (featuring you on their platform, presenting to music supervisors)

My Recommendation:
Choose non-exclusive unless the exclusive library is offering: a meaningful advance ($1,000+ per track), guaranteed placements, and a 2-year or shorter term. A 3-year exclusive with no guarantees is a bad deal.

Sync licenses that were validly issued during your publisher's term of control typically SURVIVE the reversion. This means:

- All new licensing inquiries should be directed to you

Transition Checklist:

2. Note the expiration dates of each license
3. Send formal notification to each licensee that rights administration has changed
4. Update your sync library submissions with your own contact information
5. Check if any sync licenses have automatic renewal clauses — you may need to decline renewal
6. Ensure your PRO registration reflects YOU (not the publisher) as the publisher of record

scenario: Compilation and soundtrack album licensing — contributing a track

input.user_message: A record label wants to include my song on a compilation album. What kind of license is this and what should I charge?

1. Master Use License: Permission to use YOUR specific recording on the compilation. Since you own your masters, you grant this directly.

2. Mechanical License: Permission to reproduce the underlying composition. If you are the sole songwriter, you grant this too. If there are co-writers, all composition owners must agree.

- Most legitimate labels offer a per-stream pro-rata share

Key Terms to Negotiate:
1. Non-exclusive: Your track should NOT be exclusive to this compilation. You should retain the right to release it independently and license it elsewhere.
2. Term: 3-5 years maximum. Do not grant perpetual.
3. Territory: Specify regions (worldwide or limited).
4. Digital distribution: Ensure the compilation is distributed properly so your PRO and MLC can track plays.
5. Credit: Full artist name and track title in all materials.
6. Promotional use: Limit their right to use your name/likeness in compilation promotion.

Red Flags:
- They ask for exclusive rights to your track
- No upfront payment AND no per-unit/stream royalty
- Perpetual term
- They want you to PAY to be included (this is a vanity compilation — avoid)

For a legitimate compilation at your level, I'd target a $1,000-$2,000 flat fee plus pro-rata streaming revenue. Want me to draft the license?

scenario: Licensing music for audiobooks and spoken word content

Using music as background throughout an audiobook is synchronizing your composition and recording with spoken word content. This requires a sync license (composition + master).

However, audiobooks present unique challenges:

1. Duration: Match the audiobook's commercial lifespan — 3-5 years with renewal option
2. Modifications: Allow volume adjustment and fading (necessary for speech clarity) but prohibit remixing

4. Exclusivity: Non-exclusive (you can license the same track to other audiobooks)

scenario: Advanced sync negotiation tactics — maximizing value in every deal

input.user_message: I keep getting sync offers but I feel like I'm leaving money on the table. What are the pro negotiation tactics I should be using?

expected.output_sample:
Ren, sync negotiation is a skill that separates artists earning $500 per placement from those earning $15,000. Here are the professional tactics.

1. Never Accept the First Offer:
The first offer is always a starting point, not a final number. Music supervisors and ad agencies have approved budgets with negotiation room built in. Counter at 50-100% above the initial offer — they expect this.

2. Ask 'What's the Budget?':
Before stating your price, ask the music supervisor what the project's music budget is. This prevents you from underbidding. If they say '$50,000 total for 10 songs,' you know each song should be approximately $5,000.

3. MFN (Most Favored Nations) Clause:
If your song is one of several licensed for a project, request MFN — meaning you receive the same fee as the highest-paid artist on the project. If another artist on the soundtrack negotiated $10,000, you automatically receive $10,000 too.

4. Step Deals (Escalating Fees):
Instead of a flat fee, negotiate a step deal where the fee increases based on the placement's reach:
- Initial online/social only: $3,000
- If it airs on national TV: step up to $8,000
- If it airs internationally: step up to $15,000
This gives the licensee a lower entry point while protecting your upside.

5. Separate the Rights:
- Don't bundle master + composition sync in one fee unless the total is premium
- Offer the composition license at one price and the master license at another
- Forces the buyer to see the full value of what they're licensing

6. Leverage Backend Royalties:
If the upfront fee is low, negotiate for: higher backend performance royalties (yes, you can negotiate which territories get priority cue sheet billing), guaranteed credit placement, promotional commitments (the brand promotes your music).

7. Time Pressure Works Both Ways:
Brands and productions have deadlines. If they need your music by Friday, your leverage increases. Don't be pressured into accepting — use their timeline pressure to negotiate better terms.

8. Know Your Walk-Away Number:
Before any negotiation, determine the minimum fee you'll accept. If the offer goes below that number, politely decline. Walking away from a bad deal leaves you available for a better one.

9. Build in Options:
Grant the current use but offer options for expanded use at pre-negotiated rates. Example: 'This license covers social media for 3 months. If you want to extend to TV, the pre-agreed rate is $X.' This creates future revenue without renegotiation.

At 120K monthly listeners, you have enough cultural value to command mid-tier to upper sync rates. Never undersell yourself.

scenario: Edge case: Two different companies claim to control licensing rights to the same song

input.user_message: I got a sync opportunity but now TWO different companies are claiming they control the licensing rights to my song — my old publisher and my new one. What do I do?

expected.output_sample:
Max, conflicting rights claims are a sync deal killer if not resolved quickly. Here's the immediate action plan.

Why This Happens:
- You left your old publisher, but the reversion process may not be complete
- Both publishers may have registered the song with different PROs or collection societies
- The old publisher may have a 'retention period' allowing them to collect (and license) for a period after the deal ends
- There may be a legitimate dispute about whether certain songs have actually reverted

Immediate Steps:

1. Identify the Correct Rights Holder:
Let me run check_availability on the song to see which entity is currently registered as the publisher with PROs and the MLC. I'll also use document_query to review your old and new publishing agreements and identify the contractual chain of rights.

2. Notify the Music Supervisor:
Be transparent with the supervisor about the situation. Let them know you're resolving it and provide a timeline. Music supervisors deal with this regularly — it won't kill the deal, but SILENCE will. Tell them: 'We're confirming rights administration. I'll have clarity within [X days].'

3. Resolve the Conflict:
- If the old publisher's term has definitively ended AND there's no retention period: they have no legal standing. Provide the supervisor with your reversion documentation.
- If there IS a retention period: the old publisher may still have valid licensing authority for songs administered during their term. Negotiate with them directly.
- If genuinely disputed: both parties may need to provide the supervisor with competing clearance certificates, which they cannot accept — the deal will only proceed once a single party can confirm clear title.

4. Route to Legal:
This should be flagged to the Legal agent via indii Conductor immediately. A formal cease-and-desist from your attorney to the incorrect claimant may be necessary.

Time is critical — sync opportunities have tight deadlines. The faster you resolve this, the more likely the deal survives.

scenario: Licensing music for VR experiences and metaverse platforms

input.user_message: A VR company building a metaverse concert venue wants to license my catalog for virtual performances. This is completely new to me. How should I think about this?

expected.output_sample:
Nova, metaverse/VR music licensing is an emerging frontier. The licensing frameworks are still being established, which means you have more negotiating power now than you will in 5 years when standards solidify. Here's how to approach it.

What the VR Company Needs:

1. Public Performance Rights: Virtual performances in a metaverse venue are 'public performances' — your PRO may or may not have these covered. Major PROs are still working out blanket licensing for metaverse platforms. Negotiate separately.

2. Synchronization Rights: If your music is synced to visual elements (avatar performances, virtual stage effects, interactive environments), this requires a sync license.

3. Interactive Rights: This is the NEW category. If users can interact with your music (remix in real-time, manipulate stems, trigger different sections), this goes beyond traditional sync.

License Structure Recommendation:

| Component | Fee Range | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Catalog access (non-interactive playback) | $5,000-$20,000/year | Music plays in the venue as-is |
| Interactive stem access | $10,000-$30,000/year | Users can interact with stems |
| Virtual performance events | $2,000-$10,000/event | Specific scheduled concerts |
| Avatar/digital twin rights | Negotiate heavily | Your likeness as a virtual performer |

Key Terms:
1. Duration: 1-year term maximum. This market is evolving too fast for long-term commitments.
2. Platform exclusivity: Non-exclusive — you should keep the option to license to every major metaverse platform.
3. User-generated content: Can platform users create derivative content with your music? If yes, how is it monetized and who gets paid?
4. Data/analytics: Require the platform to share usage data with you — how many plays, how many users engaged, peak times.
5. NFT/tokenization: If the platform creates NFTs or tokens using your music, you need separate rights and compensation.

The metaverse concert market is projected to grow significantly through 2030. Getting in now with favorable non-exclusive terms positions you for long-term revenue as these platforms scale.
